refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work performed.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The system's functional role is to provide visibility into energy consumption in a form that allows stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality) to assess whether operational practices meet the target. The target achievement is a property of the integrated system-in-use, not a function the NGHE solution alone must perform. This refinement is solution-neutral: it does not prescribe sensors, data pipelines, or algorithms—only that energy and work metrics must be measurable and reportable.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides a mechanism (e.g., telemetry interface, dashboard, data export) through which energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved can be computed and retrieved for any agreed evaluation period. Confirm that the reported values are traceable to underlying sensor data and work logs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer the quantity of work performed (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or equivalent unit).
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and expose the ratio of energy consumed to work performed over a specified evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide access to energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality) for review and decision-making.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric post-collection.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into the equipment control loop; calculate energy_per_ton continuously during operation and store snapshots at defined intervals.

DP3: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: (a) energy metering at the power distribution point (centralized), (b) work quantification via independent load/displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps and compute ratios offline.

DP4: Integrate energy and work measurement into a unified telemetry module that samples both streams at synchronized intervals, applies calibration transforms, and emits energy_per_ton records with metadata (timestamp, operating mode, environmental conditions).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: (a) energy metering at the power distribution point (centralized), (b) work quantification via independent load/displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps and compute energy_per_ton offline with full traceability.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization drift between energy and work channels could introduce systematic error if not bounded; recommend synchronized clock or relative timing validation.
Calibration coupling: if energy and work sensors share a single calibration reference, loss of that reference affects both; recommend independent calibration chains.

Data pipeline coupling: if offline computation is tightly bound to a single data format or storage system, portability and auditability suffer; recommend schema versioning and format-agnostic interfaces.

Environmental coupling: energy measurement may be sensitive to ambient temperature or humidity; work measurement may be sensitive to surface conditions; recommend environmental monitoring as a separate concern.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves logical independence by separating energy measurement (power domain) from work measurement (mechanical domain) at the sensor level. Offline computation decouples real-time control from reporting, reducing latency coupling. Dual-channel design allows independent validation and fault detection. Synchronization and calibration are identified as integration points requiring explicit design; these are manageable with clear interface contracts.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is accessible for instrumentation (e.g., fuel flow, electrical bus) without disrupting safety or operations. RISK: If energy source is embedded or inaccessible, DP3 may require redesign to DP2 (embedded measurement).

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) can be measured independently of energy input (e.g., via load cells, GPS displacement, or material tracking). RISK: If work is only inferable from energy (e.g., in highly coupled hydraulic systems), independence is lost; recommend early validation with equipment OEM.

ASSUMPTION: Offline computation is acceptable for reporting (i.e., real-time energy_per_ton feedback is not required for operational control). RISK: If real-time feedback is needed for adaptive control, DP3 must be augmented with DP2 (embedded real-time calculation).

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization can be achieved to within acceptable error bounds (recommend ±1% of measurement interval). RISK: If clock drift exceeds tolerance, systematic bias in energy_per_ton will accumulate; recommend periodic re-synchronization and drift monitoring.

ASSUMPTION: Calibration standards and traceability are available for both energy and work sensors. RISK: If calibration is unavailable or expensive, measurement credibility is compromised; recommend early engagement with metrology and standards bodies.

ASSUMPTION: Data storage and retrieval infrastructure supports the volume and frequency of dual-channel logging over the evaluation period. RISK: If storage is undersized or unreliable, data loss could invalidate acceptance criteria; recommend capacity planning and redundancy.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits installation of measurement sensors without introducing new hazards. RISK: If sensor installation creates pinch points, electrical hazards, or obstructs operator visibility, safety review may reject DP3; recommend early safety assessment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement of energy consumption and work performed, with synchronized dual-channel data collection (energy at source, work at output), and shall support offline computation of energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved with full traceability, calibration metadata, and timestamp validation to satisfy acceptance criteria ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period. DP3 is recommended as the primary design approach, with contingency to DP2 (embedded real-time calculation) if offline computation is deemed operationally unacceptable. Explicit design tasks: (1) Define sensor placement and accessibility constraints in collaboration with equipment OEM and safety team; (2) Establish timestamp synchronization protocol and acceptable drift bounds; (3) Specify calibration chain and traceability requirements; (4) Design data schema with versioning and format-agnostic serialization; (5) Validate independence of energy and work measurement channels through early prototype testing.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement architecture encompassing energy metering at power distribution point, work quantification via load/displacement sensors, timestamp synchronization, and offline energy_per_ton computation with full traceability logging.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit synchronized sensor readings (energy, load, displacement, timestamps) and computed energy_per_ton metrics to external monitoring/analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive work task definitions and operational parameters that define the scope for energy and work quantification.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate sensor data, synchronization signals, and computed results across distributed measurement points if multi-site deployment is required.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: NGHE Solution is the natural home for energy metering, sensor integration, data synchronization, and offline computation. The dual-channel architecture (centralized energy + independent load/displacement) aligns with NGHE's role as the integrated measurement and analytics backbone. Minor diffusion: timestamp synchronization may require coordination with Vehicle for sensor clock alignment, but logic remains centralized.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a platform/asset, not a measurement system. Load and displacement sensors may be mounted on the vehicle, but the dual-channel architecture, energy metering at power distribution, synchronization logic, and offline computation belong in NGHE Solution. Placing DP here would scatter measurement logic across vehicle firmware and NGHE, creating tight coupling and maintenance burden.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a measurement system. Energy metering, sensor fusion, and offline computation have no logical home in teleoperation. Significant diffusion: would require teleoperation to manage measurement infrastructure, violating separation of concerns.

Very poor containment: Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection and safety alerts, not energy/work quantification. While energy metrics could inform safety thresholds, the measurement architecture itself does not belong in Safety Services. High diffusion: would entangle measurement logic with safety logic, complicating both.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The dual-channel measurement architecture (DP3) is fundamentally a data acquisition, synchronization, and analytics function. NGHE Solution is the designated system-level measurement and intelligence layer, responsible for integrating heterogeneous sensor inputs, performing offline computation, and maintaining traceability. This module naturally contains the energy metering subsystem, sensor fusion logic, timestamp synchronization, and energy_per_ton calculation. Vehicle provides the physical mounting points for sensors; Teleoperation and Safety Services consume the resulting metrics via Telemetry interface. This separation preserves cohesion and minimizes coupling.
containment_risks: Sensor clock drift: If Vehicle-mounted load/displacement sensors lack synchronized clocks, NGHE Solution must implement clock correction logic or require Vehicle to provide timestamped sensor readings. Mitigation: define sensor interface contract to include timestamps from Vehicle.

Real-time vs. offline tension: DP3 specifies offline computation, but if real-time energy alerts are needed for safety, NGHE Solution may need to compute energy_per_ton incrementally. Mitigation: clarify whether SafetyAlert interface requires real-time energy thresholds or if offline post-analysis suffices.

Power distribution metering location: If power distribution point is physically on Vehicle, NGHE Solution must establish a reliable data link to retrieve energy readings. Mitigation: define Network Link protocol for energy meter communication.

Traceability logging overhead: Full traceability requires persistent storage of all sensor readings and computation steps. NGHE Solution must manage storage capacity and data retention policies. Mitigation: establish data lifecycle and archival strategy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by specifying a dual-channel measurement architecture (centralized energy metering + independent work quantification) with synchronization and offline computation. The DP is concrete, measurable, and traceable to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). The offline computation with full traceability aligns with the need for rigorous energy accounting.

diffusion_risks: Sensor clock synchronization: Load/displacement sensors mounted on Vehicle may lack synchronized clocks. If Vehicle firmware provides unsynchronized readings, NGHE Solution must implement clock correction logic. Risk: timestamp accuracy degradation if Vehicle clock drift exceeds acceptable bounds. Mitigation: define sensor interface contract requiring Vehicle to provide timestamped readings or expose clock reference.

Power distribution metering location ambiguity: If energy meter is physically located on Vehicle, NGHE Solution depends on a reliable Network Link to retrieve readings. Risk: data loss or latency if Vehicle-to-NGHE communication fails. Mitigation: specify redundancy and failsafe behavior for energy meter data link.

Real-time vs. offline computation tension: DP3 specifies offline energy_per_ton computation, but Safety Services may require real-time energy thresholds for hazard detection. Risk: if real-time alerts are needed, NGHE Solution must compute incrementally, complicating the offline-first design. Mitigation: clarify whether SafetyAlert interface requires real-time energy metrics or if offline post-analysis suffices.

Traceability logging scope: Full traceability requires persistent storage of all sensor readings and computation steps. Risk: storage capacity and data retention policies not yet defined. Mitigation: establish data lifecycle strategy and storage capacity planning.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution exhibits excellent containment (0.92 score) as the natural home for the dual-channel measurement architecture. Energy metering, sensor fusion, timestamp synchronization, and offline computation are cohesive measurement and analytics functions. Vehicle provides physical sensor mounting points but does not own measurement logic. Teleoperation and Safety Services consume metrics via Telemetry interface, preserving separation of concerns. Minor diffusion risk is localized to sensor clock alignment, which can be mitigated via interface contract.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from DP3 outputs. NGHE Solution's offline computation with full traceability enables post-evaluation verification: energy readings from power distribution meter and work quantification from load/displacement sensors can be audited against logged computation steps. Timestamp synchronization ensures temporal alignment for accurate energy_per_ton calculation. Verification requires: (1) energy meter calibration and validation, (2) load/displacement sensor accuracy confirmation, (3) synchronization tolerance specification, (4) traceability log audit trail.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated as the owner of DP3. External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link inout) are well-defined. Vehicle provides sensor inputs via Network Link; Teleoperation and Safety Services consume energy metrics via Telemetry. Ownership boundaries are explicit and minimize coupling. Remaining ambiguities are operational (clock synchronization protocol, real-time vs. offline trade-offs, data retention policy) rather than architectural.

recommended_revisions: Specify sensor interface contract: Define whether Vehicle-mounted load/displacement sensors must provide timestamped readings or if NGHE Solution will apply clock correction. Include acceptable clock drift tolerance (e.g., ±100 ms).

Clarify real-time vs. offline requirement: Confirm whether SafetyAlert interface requires real-time energy thresholds or if offline energy_per_ton analysis suffices. If real-time alerts are needed, revise DP3 to include incremental computation logic.

Define power distribution metering protocol: Specify Network Link protocol for energy meter communication (e.g., CAN, Ethernet, wireless), including redundancy and failsafe behavior if Vehicle-to-NGHE link is lost.

Establish data lifecycle and storage strategy: Define retention period for traceability logs, storage capacity requirements, and archival policy. Specify whether logs are retained on NGHE Solution or offloaded to external storage.

Validate timestamp synchronization mechanism: Specify synchronization method (e.g., NTP, PTP, GPS) and acceptable tolerance for energy_per_ton calculation accuracy. Include acceptance criteria for synchronization error (e.g., ±50 ms).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP3 (dual-channel measurement architecture with offline computation) is concrete, traceable, and well-contained within NGHE Solution. However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal critical operational and interface ambiguities that must be resolved before detailed design proceeds. Given high safety criticality, these ambiguities warrant decomposition of DP3 into sub-level FRs that explicitly address: (1) sensor synchronization and clock alignment, (2) real-time vs. offline computation trade-off, (3) power distribution metering protocol and failsafe behavior, and (4) traceability logging scope and data lifecycle. Decomposing now ensures that interface contracts, synchronization tolerances, and failsafe strategies are captured as explicit functional requirements rather than deferred to implementation. This approach reduces downstream rework and safety-critical integration risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Synchronize load/displacement sensor timestamps with energy meter readings to within acceptable tolerance (±50 ms) to enable accurate energy_per_ton calculation.
FR3.2: Retrieve energy meter readings from Vehicle via Network Link with redundancy and failsafe behavior to ensure data availability even if primary communication link is degraded or lost.

FR3.3: Determine whether real-time energy thresholds are required for SafetyAlert interface or if offline energy_per_ton analysis suffices; if real-time alerts are needed, compute incremental energy metrics with bounded latency.
FR3.4: Log all sensor readings, computation steps, and energy_per_ton results with full traceability to enable post-evaluation verification and audit trail.
FR3.5: Define data lifecycle and storage strategy for traceability logs, including retention period, storage capacity, and archival policy.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound but operationally underspecified. Decomposition into sub-FRs will drive DP refinement at the next level, ensuring that sensor synchronization protocol, failsafe metering strategy, real-time vs. offline computation decision, and traceability logging scope are explicitly designed rather than assumed. This is critical for high-safety-criticality systems where interface contracts and failsafe behavior must be rigorous.

assumptions: Vehicle-mounted load/displacement sensors can provide timestamped readings or expose a clock reference for NGHE Solution to apply clock correction.
Network Link between Vehicle and NGHE Solution supports redundant communication paths or failsafe fallback (e.g., cached energy meter readings).
SafetyAlert interface does not require real-time energy thresholds; offline energy_per_ton analysis is acceptable for hazard detection. (This assumption must be validated via FR3.3 decomposition.)
NGHE Solution has sufficient storage capacity to retain traceability logs for the operational lifetime of the system or a defined retention period.
Timestamp synchronization tolerance of ±50 ms is acceptable for energy_per_ton calculation accuracy. (Tolerance must be validated against energy accounting requirements.)
open_risks: Sensor clock synchronization: If Vehicle firmware does not provide synchronized timestamps or clock reference, NGHE Solution must implement complex clock correction logic, increasing implementation complexity and potential for synchronization errors.
Power distribution metering data loss: If Vehicle-to-NGHE Network Link fails, energy meter readings may be lost or delayed, compromising energy accounting accuracy and traceability.

Real-time vs. offline computation tension: If SafetyAlert interface requires real-time energy thresholds, DP3's offline-first design must be revised to include incremental computation, complicating the architecture and increasing computational load on NGHE Solution.
Traceability logging storage: Full traceability logging may exceed available storage capacity, requiring offloading to external storage or data compression strategies not yet defined.

Synchronization tolerance validation: Acceptable clock drift tolerance (±50 ms) must be validated against energy_per_ton calculation accuracy requirements; if tolerance is too loose, energy accounting errors may exceed acceptable bounds.
evidence_needed: Vehicle interface specification: Define sensor interface contract, including whether load/displacement sensors provide timestamped readings, clock reference exposure, and acceptable clock drift tolerance.
Network Link redundancy specification: Define failsafe behavior for energy meter data link, including redundancy strategy, data caching, and recovery protocol if primary link is lost.
SafetyAlert interface requirements: Clarify whether real-time energy thresholds are required for hazard detection or if offline energy_per_ton analysis suffices.
Storage capacity analysis: Estimate traceability logging volume (sensor readings, computation steps, energy_per_ton results) and validate against available storage capacity on NGHE Solution.
Synchronization tolerance validation: Perform sensitivity analysis to determine acceptable clock drift tolerance (±50 ms or tighter) based on energy_per_ton calculation accuracy requirements.
Data lifecycle policy: Define retention period for traceability logs, archival strategy, and compliance requirements (e.g., regulatory audit trail retention).
